planting.note: Plant pseudobulb divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, strongly to moderately acidic (pH 5.0–6.5) soil in full shade to part shade; discolor calanthe is a Japanese native terrestrial orchid and one of the hardiest of all cultivatable orchids, surviving winters to zone 5 when grown in protected, well-drained woodland conditions.

directions: Plant pseudobulb divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, strongly to moderately acidic (pH 5.0–6.5), well-drained woodland soil in full shade; allow divisions to establish for a full season before expecting bloom. Calanthe discolor (discolor calanthe) is one of the hardiest and most gardener-friendly of all terrestrial orchids — a Japanese and East Asian species that can be grown as a perennial in the shade garden from zone 5 through zone 9, emerging in spring with attractive, pleated, semi-evergreen basal leaves and producing elegant, upright flower spikes 10–18 inches tall bearing 12–20 graceful, two-toned, purple-and-white orchid flowers in April–May. The contrast between the purplish-brown outer tepals and the pure-white lip (labellum) is striking and distinctive. Unlike epiphytic orchids (which grow in bark mixes), calanthes grow in humus-rich, acidic woodland soil and are treated much like hostas — planted in shaded beds, left to establish, and allowed to slowly clump up over years. In the shade garden, they bloom at an important transition period (April–May) before most summer shade perennials reach peak. C. discolor has been used extensively in breeding cold-hardy terrestrial orchid hybrids.
sources: University of Georgia Cooperative Extension — Hardy Orchids

planting.note: Plant dormant tubers or container divisions in spring in moist to wet, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to part shade; fragrant ladies tresses is a native North American terrestrial orchid of wet meadows, marsh edges, and moist open woodlands that is remarkably adaptable as a garden plant, naturalizing in moist sites.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; keep consistently moist. Spiranthes odorata (fragrant ladies tresses) is a native North American terrestrial orchid of the southeastern coastal plain — one of the most gardener-accessible native orchids available for cultivation, native to moist meadows, pond margins, and marsh edges from Maryland through Florida and Texas. It produces a slender, erect spike 10–24 inches tall bearing tiny, white, sweetly vanilla-scented flowers arranged in a characteristic spiral pattern (the 'ladies tresses' that gives the genus its name) from September through November — an unusually late bloom time that makes it exceptional for extending the season in native plant gardens. The 'Chadds Ford' selection is particularly vigorous and easy in garden conditions. Unlike many native orchids that are difficult to transplant and resent disturbance, S. odorata is relatively adaptable and can be established in rain gardens, moist meadows, and pond margins where consistent moisture is available. The fragrance of the fall-blooming spikes is notable for its sweet vanilla quality. An underused native orchid for moist southern gardens.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Native Orchids

planting.note: Plant container stock or seeds in spring in moist, well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; money tree is a Central American wetland tree that tolerates significant flooding and poor drainage (naturally growing along streams and floodplains), making it excellent for consistently moist to wet sites in tropical gardens.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; money tree tolerates periodic flooding and poor drainage. Pachira aquatica (money tree or Malabar chestnut) is a Central American tropical tree famous in two quite different contexts: as a beloved ornamental houseplant (braided trunk forms sold under the 'money tree' name, associated in Feng Shui with prosperity) and as a significant wild and semi-cultivated food tree in its native tropical Central and South American habitat. The tree produces large, distinctive, 5-to-7-fingered, palmate leaves and spectacular, large (8–12 inch), creamy-white flowers with hundreds of long, red-tipped, brush-like stamens — one of the showiest flowers of any tropical tree. The large, woody fruits contain edible seeds (Malabar chestnuts) with a nutty flavor when roasted or boiled — eaten throughout Central America as a nutritious food. Braided trunk forms (produced by braiding multiple young seedlings while soft) have become extremely popular houseplants worldwide. In zone 10–12, money tree grows as a large landscape tree; in zone 9, it requires frost protection.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tropical Trees
Purdue University — New Crop Resources — Pachira aquatica

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile, deep soil in part shade to full sun; jade vine is a Philippine rainforest species adapted to the dappled shade of forest edges and is most free-flowering with at least 6 hours of sun — it is extremely rare in cultivation outside of specialty botanical gardens.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil in bright indirect light to full sun; provide very strong, substantial support structure — jade vine becomes massive. Strongylodon macrobotrys (jade vine or tayabak) is one of the most spectacularly beautiful and rarest flowering vines in cultivation — a Philippine rainforest legume producing extraordinary, cascading, 3-to-5-foot racemes of 75–100 large, claw-shaped, iridescent jade-blue-to-turquoise-green flowers whose unique color is essentially unmatched by any other flower. The jade-green flowers have an almost translucent, gemstone-like quality in sunlight. Jade vine is critically endangered in its native Philippine rainforest habitat due to deforestation and the disruption of its specialized bat and sunbird pollinators. The vine is massive and powerful in ideal conditions — growing to 60–70 feet and requiring very substantial support. It blooms in late winter to spring after a cool dry period. It is extraordinarily difficult to grow outside of tropical conditions (zone 11–12) and is primarily a botanical garden specimen; very rarely available in the nursery trade. For true tropical enthusiasts only. An extraordinary rarity of the plant world.
sources: University of Hawaii Cooperative Extension — Tropical Vines
International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN)

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container stock in spring in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; frangipani is one of the most iconic tropical flowering trees for Gulf Coast and subtropical gardens and performs well in zone 9–12 when given good drainage and full sun.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; allow soil to dry between waterings. Plumeria rubra (frangipani) is one of the most beloved and iconic tropical flowering trees in the world — a deciduous Central American tree or large shrub producing clusters of breathtakingly beautiful, intensely fragrant, 5-petaled flowers in an enormous range of colors (red, pink, orange, yellow, white, salmon, and multicolor) from late spring through fall on bare or nearly bare branches, with the flowers most concentrated when the plant is otherwise leafless. The legendary fragrance of frangipani — sweet, heady, exotic — has made it the quintessential tropical flower scent; the white-and-yellow P. obtusa and related species are used for Hawaiian leis, Buddhist temple offerings, and perfume across Southeast Asia. In zone 9, plumeria grows as a large shrub (6–10 feet) and goes fully dormant and leafless in winter — it should not be watered when leafless to avoid root rot. Rooted cuttings are the fastest way to bloom. Plumeria is surprisingly drought-tolerant and requires perfect drainage to avoid stem rot.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Plumeria
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Tropical Plants
